title: Aglio e Olio
tagline: Naples' one-pan pasta — garlic, good olive oil, chilli, and a splash of pasta water.
macroNotes: Authentic aglio e olio is deliberately protein-poor — it's mostly pasta and oil. Per serving is ~620 kcal, most of it from the olive oil.
To make it a gym meal, top with 150 g grilled/pan-seared chicken breast (~50 g protein for 2) or toss in a handful of prawns at the garlic stage. Soya chunks don't belong in this delicate dish.
Cut ~150 kcal per serving by dropping the olive oil to 3 tbsp total and leaning harder on pasta water for the silky finish.

items: 1 tbsp salt for the pasta water
Reserve ~1/2 cup (120 ml) starchy pasta water before draining
4-5 tbsp extra virgin olive oil (use the best you have — it carries the whole dish)
1 to 1.5 tsp red chilli flakes (to taste)
1 tbsp butter (optional — richness boost if your olive oil is a cheap, low-aroma one)
Small bunch fresh parsley (~2 tbsp chopped), optional but traditional
Salt to taste (be generous at the end if you under-salted the water)

steps.title: Salt the water and boil
steps.body: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil and add 1 tbsp salt — salt it like the sea, this is the pasta's only chance to season from within. Add 200 g spaghetti and cook until al dente (1 minute less than the packet says). If you forgot to salt the water, don't panic — you'll salt the pasta directly when tossing.

steps.title: Reserve pasta water
steps.body: Before draining, scoop out ~1/2 cup (120 ml) of the starchy pasta water. This starch is what emulsifies the oil into a silky sauce — do not skip it.

steps.title: Gently cook the garlic
steps.body: While the pasta cooks, heat 4-5 tbsp olive oil in a wide pan over low to medium-low heat. Add the 6-8 sliced garlic cloves and cook gently 2-3 minutes until just light golden — never brown. Browned garlic turns bitter and ruins the dish. If your olive oil is cheap and low-aroma, drop in 1 tbsp butter here for richness.

steps.title: Bloom the chilli
steps.body: Stir in 1 to 1.5 tsp red chilli flakes and cook just 20-30 seconds to release the aroma. Keep the heat low so nothing burns.

steps.title: Toss with pasta water
steps.body: Add the drained spaghetti straight into the pan along with a splash of the reserved pasta water. Toss hard and continuously over medium heat for about 1-2 minutes — the sauce should turn glossy and cling to the strands, not sit as oil in the bottom. Add more pasta water a splash at a time until it's silky, not oily. If the water was unsalted, sprinkle salt directly now and taste.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.body: Turn off the heat, toss in ~2 tbsp chopped parsley and a pinch of black pepper if using. Taste a strand and adjust salt and chilli. Plate immediately while it's shiny and hot.

notes: Traditionally no cheese goes in aglio e olio. If you must, grate a little Parmigiano-Reggiano — but never soft mozzarella, which clumps and stretches into a sticky ball in the hot pasta. If you only have mozzarella, tear a few small bits over the plate after serving instead of tossing it in.

Cheap olive oil trick: gently warm 5 tbsp oil with 2 lightly smashed garlic cloves over low heat for 4-5 minutes (just tiny bubbles, no browning), rest 5 minutes, fish out the cloves — then cook the sliced garlic in that now-infused oil.
Scales cleanly: 100 g pasta + 3-4 garlic cloves + ~2.5 tbsp oil per person. For 500 g pasta use 12-16 cloves and 10-12 tbsp oil.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
